video_title: What Happens When Companies Don’t Need People | Fernando Nikolić
gist: McCormack and Fernando Nikolić (founder of crypto-narrative data firm Perception) describe a hands-on 'AI psychosis' phase in which solo operators now build software that previously needed 15-person agencies, argue most SaaS and design tools are being commoditized to zero while proprietary hard-to-gather data remains the durable moat, and debate the downstream consequences for jobs, company structure, democracy, and Bitcoin's narrower-than-promised future.

themes.theme: Solo-operator economics & the death of agencies/SaaS

insights.claim: AI coding tools let a single non-engineer build, in roughly two weeks, an operations/CMS/email/inventory system that McCormack estimates previously required 15 people, 18 months, and ~£1M from an agency.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: If a single person captures 99%+ cost compression on custom software, valuations of dev agencies, low-end SaaS, and website builders face structural compression — short the commoditized tooling layer.
insights.quote: It would have taken 15 people a year and a half and a million pound bill. I'd build it on my own, no help. In two weeks.

insights.claim: Front-end/UI coding and design have been commoditized to near-zero value because 'everything is code' — video, animation, CMS, and back end can all be generated by an agent on demand.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Revenue tied to selling design/front-end labor is structurally impaired; value migrates to whoever controls proprietary data and taste.
insights.quote: UI's front end coding is just dead in terms of it's commoditized and the value has gone down to zero. And you start to realize that everything is code.

insights.claim: A solo AI-built business can run at ~94% profit margins because there is no team, no VC, and no agency bill — Fernando left an executive job and recovered ~90% of his salary within 8 months as a one-man company.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: The marginal cost of launching a software business has collapsed to ~$200/month, undercutting the VC-funded startup model and the value of seed-stage capital itself.
insights.quote: I have a profit margin of like 94%.

insights.claim: Specific incumbent tools are flagged as losing their moat: Squarespace (no moat), Canva, Grammarly, and even Adobe/Photoshop are framed as obsolescing as users generate equivalents on demand.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Watch ARR retention and pricing power at single-feature SaaS names; the 'SaaS apocalypse' thesis argues custom-built internal tools replace subscriptions even if buggier.
insights.quote: The realization that Adobe no longer has a business. AI is crazy.

insights.claim: The winning model is to ship intentionally buggy internal tools and reserve weekly time to fix them, accepting lower polish in exchange for eliminating thousands of dollars in monthly SaaS subscriptions.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Total addressable market for mid-tier B2B SaaS shrinks as companies internalize tooling; durable SaaS must offer data or standards a vibe-coder can't replicate.

insights.claim: Custom AI-built software is so company-specific that productizing it as SaaS is pointless; the distribution model becomes open-source-style — hand over the artifact and let the buyer's AI ingest and customize it.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Software distribution may shift from licensed SaaS to forkable templates, undercutting recurring-revenue software multiples.
insights.quote: there's no point me trying to make a SAS version of this because it's custom to how we do things.

insights.claim: The remaining defensible moat in an AI-commoditized world is hard, tedious, or expensive-to-gather proprietary data — e.g., installing 400 physical cameras to capture real-world data is something you cannot vibe-code.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Investors should value AI-era businesses on the difficulty/exclusivity of their data acquisition pipeline, not on their software stack which is now replicable.
insights.quote: there is that mode is in the data that is like hard, tedious or expensive.

insights.claim: Fernando's company Perception sells cleaned, enriched data and crypto-narrative intelligence delivered via dashboards, APIs, and MCP connections, and is running 5–10 sales demos a week to AI-forward digital-asset firms.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: MCP-delivered data feeds are an emerging monetization channel; data vendors that plug directly into agent workflows can capture spend companies would otherwise use to hire data staff.
insights.quote: they connect to that data through the MCP. So, it's one company gathering a bunch of data from a lot of insane sources that are expensive, tedious, and hard to do it yourself.

insights.claim: Well-funded companies increasingly prefer to outsource clean data rather than hire a 'data guy,' validating data-as-a-service over headcount even when they vibe-code the rest of their stack.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Demand shifts from data-engineering hires to data vendors — a tailwind for clean-data providers and a headwind for in-house data roles.

themes.theme: Agent orchestration as the operating skill

insights.claim: Current frontier coding agents behave like an 80%-accurate mid-level developer: they interpret a brief ~80% right, deliver ~80% right, sometimes regress prior work or go rogue, and may need ~5 runs to get one task correct.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Sets a realistic productivity ceiling — output is gated by human QA/orchestration bandwidth, so 'autonomous' agent claims should be discounted for rework loops.
insights.quote: it develops like a mid-level developer... it interprets the brief about 80% right.

insights.claim: Because code outcomes are binary (works or doesn't), agents can be run autonomously with a strong 'harness' that escalates only on real failures and routes minor data issues to a second fixer-agent rather than the human.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: The defensible operator skill is building reliable harnesses/escalation logic; this is where durable productivity (and product differentiation) lives.
insights.quote: Only let me know when things are burning and not working the way they're supposed to.

insights.claim: Fernando standardizes on Google Gemini for nearly everything, pays ~$200/month for Google AI Ultra (cut from $250 to compete with Claude's 20x Max plan), and tests local models (Ollama + Gemma) as a cost hedge.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Frontier-model pricing is already competing down (Google cut Ultra to undercut Anthropic), and power users probe local models as substitution risk to subscription revenue.
insights.quote: it was 250 a month is now gone down to 200 to be more competitive with the claw 20x max plan.

insights.claim: Heavy Claude usage blows past the $200 tier — McCormack exhausts his Claude 20x Max allotment by Sunday after a Friday refill and expects to spend at least $10K in inference this month.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Real power-user inference spend can be 50x the headline subscription, signaling meaningful usage-based revenue upside for model providers from the top cohort.
insights.quote: I reckon this month I'll do 10 grand at least.

insights.claim: Pre-built agent harnesses like Gary Tan / Y Combinator's 'GBrain/GStack' act as a CTO-grade reviewer that can give a downloaded user skills equivalent to 6–8 months of human onboarding in a day.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Forkable expert 'skills' compress the learning curve, accelerating the supply of capable solo builders and intensifying competition in software-enabled niches.

insights.claim: AI is strong at binary code tasks but genuinely bad at non-code language work — strategy, opinion, brand positioning, marketing copy — producing homogenized 'slop' that strips out edge and flair.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Taste, voice, and judgment remain scarce and monetizable; this is the explicit gap Perception sells into (narrative engineering), and a caution against over-automating brand functions.
insights.quote: those are things that I believe AI is really bad at. And it's really really bad.

themes.theme: Labor displacement & the shape of the firm

insights.claim: Block/Square is cited as laying off ~4,000 people (~40% of workforce) to focus on AI, and when probed in a demo, such firms' actual AI use amounted to little more than 'we have Claude.'
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: A gap between AI layoff narratives and shallow actual deployment implies either over-firing risk or a large unexploited productivity runway — watch which firms actually rebuild around agents vs. just buy seats.
insights.quote: Square laid off 4,000 people. So 40% of their workforce.

insights.claim: The company of the future may collapse toward a small board of decision-makers (e.g., ~25 people) with agents doing execution, raising the open question of what the displaced majority does.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: If org size compresses toward a decision-making core, labor-heavy services valuations and employment-linked consumption assumptions need rethinking.
insights.quote: Does does a company just become a board of decision makers?

insights.claim: Both speakers expect a near-term hostile public backlash against AI analogous to historical resistance to cars/automation, and frame avoiding a 'permanent underclass' as the central social risk.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: Anticipate regulatory and political pressure (AI taxation, redistribution) as a sector risk; positioning may hinge on how productivity gains are distributed.
insights.quote: I think what we're going to face is a hostile reaction to AI and it's going to be soon.

insights.claim: The core macro risk is distribution: exponential AI productivity captured by a wealthy class could leave everyone else unable to even afford tokens/inference, a 'Black Mirror' scenario of ad-gated or labor-gated AI access.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Token affordability could become a new utility-bill line item and a political flashpoint; access models (ad-supported tiers) may emerge as a monetization frontier.
insights.quote: Will they able to be able to afford the tokens, the inference?

insights.claim: LLMs are framed as the next universal utility — alongside electricity, internet, water — with every home and company eventually carrying a recurring 'AI bill.'
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Supports a long-duration, recurring-revenue view of inference demand, favoring compute/energy infrastructure as a utility-like growth vector.
insights.quote: every company's gonna have AI and every home's going to have AI... you're going to have your AI bill.

insights.claim: Mass-market AI adoption is shallow — ChatGPT has ~1 billion users but most treat it as a Google replacement (e.g., a Gemini TV ad showing Harry Kane timing a barbecue), not as a business-building tool.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: The productivity-revolution thesis is concentrated in a tiny high-agency cohort; mass usage monetizes more like search/ads than like enterprise transformation.
insights.quote: It's a replacement for Google.

themes.theme: Information asymmetry, media disruption & content economics

insights.claim: AI is framed as the latest in a lineage of asymmetry-destroying technologies — printing press, file-sharing (Napster/Pirate Bay), Bitcoin — collapsing the middlemen whose entire reason to exist was controlling access to information.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: A unifying lens for shorting gatekeeper business models (education, media, agencies) whose value rested on information asymmetry now being dissolved.
insights.quote: All of these institutions or third parties that the the entire reason to exist was based on this information asymmetry.

insights.claim: AI-decentralized creation could break Hollywood/Netflix's formula-driven economics: a solo creator might only need to sell 50–100 copies to recoup, enabling a long tail of high-quality film and music discovered via ratings.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Streaming incumbents' content-cost moats erode if low-budget AI-native creators capture cultural attention — watch creator-led IP and discovery/ratings platforms.

insights.claim: The 'Backrooms' film (a 4chan-meme origin by a ~20-year-old YouTube-native director) is cited as grossing ~$300M on a ~$10M budget, evidence that meme/creator-native IP can outperform studio development.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: 30x budget-to-gross outcomes from creator-native IP argue for backing distributed creator pipelines over traditional studio greenlighting.
insights.quote: is now in cinemas and making 300 million I think on a 10 million budget.

insights.claim: The attention algorithm structurally penalizes nuance: a Bitcoin-as-best-money episode 'does okay' while an AI-doom episode 'blows up,' trapping creators in a feedback loop of doubling down on one opinion to retain audience.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Engagement incentives systematically degrade information quality; a real opening exists for tools/platforms that reward accuracy over outrage.
insights.quote: If I talk about all the robots are going to kill us all... it's going to blow up.

insights.claim: A counter-trend of physical/analog media revival (Gen Z buying DVDs, vinyl, doing TikTok 'BookTok' reviews of Marcus Aurelius/The Iliad) is emerging as a reaction to sanitized, homogenized digital experiences.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: A niche but real demand for discoverability and analog scarcity hints at monetizable nostalgia/curation products against algorithmic feeds.

insights.claim: McCormack built 'show us receipts' — an AI system tracking 628 UK MPs that ingested ~10,915 tweets/Bluesky posts, fact-checks them against primary sources at ~81% accuracy, and scores MPs and parties on truthfulness.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Demonstrates AI-driven civic-accountability tooling is now buildable solo; the constraints are legal (UK libel) and cost, not capability.
insights.quote: Tracking 628 MPs. I ingested 10,000 915 tweets. Got a 81 out of 100% accuracy on them.

insights.claim: The economics of large-scale fact-checking are still meaningful: processing ~10,000 tweets cost ~£3,000, making a full historical backfill a ~£30,000 decision.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Inference cost remains a real gating factor for data-heavy AI products — a concrete unit-economics datapoint for anyone modeling LLM-based analytics businesses.
insights.quote: to process 10,000 tweets cost me about three grand.

insights.claim: Edge cases (e.g., misattributing a retweet as the MP's own false claim) and UK libel exposure are blocking release, illustrating that the hard last-mile of AI products is accuracy/legal liability, not the build.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Liability and edge-case accuracy are the real moats and risks for AI-truth products — relevant to any venture deploying automated claims against named parties.

insights.claim: Proposed next steps — ingesting all public government contracts to map awards against awarders' Facebook friendships, and fact-checking parliamentary 'Question Time' — frame AI as a corruption/waste-detection layer for the public sector.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: Points to a civic-tech / govtech opportunity: automated, real-time public-records analysis to surface waste and conflicts of interest at scale.
insights.quote: I want the black mirror version of the public sector where all information is public.

insights.claim: Fernando is openly 'Bitcoin blackpilled': hyperbitcoinization and Bitcoin overtaking the USD as global reserve has failed, and Bitcoin will remain an important niche cohort akin to gold bugs rather than a fiat-killer.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: A prominent long-time Bitcoin advocate downgrading the maximalist thesis to 'niche store-of-value' is a notable sentiment signal for positioning expectations around BTC.
insights.quote: in terms of like this like ethos that a lot of Bitcoiners like to say about hyper bitcoinization... Yeah, that is failed.

insights.claim: Fernando argues fiat money is necessary alongside Bitcoin: a pure Bitcoin standard would make everyone too risk-averse to fund speculative, unprofitable-for-years startups, so 'dirty press-of-a-button money' enables innovation.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: A heterodox-for-Bitcoiners view that fiat and BTC coexist permanently — reframes BTC as a personal/family hedge rather than a monetary endgame.
insights.quote: we need fiat. We need dirty press of a button money to actually make stuff happen.

insights.claim: Bitcoin's real utility is reframed as a teaching tool for why inflation is bad rather than as an inflation hedge itself; adoption stalls because most people emotionally refuse the worldview shift it requires.
insights.speaker: Peter McCormack
insights.so_what: If BTC's value is pedagogical/cultural rather than transactional, adoption forecasts and TAM models built on mass monetary use are overstated.
insights.quote: it's not that Bitcoin is anti-inflationary, but it's a tool to teach people why inflation is bad.

insights.claim: Argentina's Milei is credited with fixing inflation and normalizing prudent monetary debate; Fernando expects re-election and notes the change is 'palpable' because people can now save and plan, with Latin America broadly trending right-wing.
insights.speaker: Fernando Nikolić
insights.so_what: A constructive read on Argentine reform durability and a regional rightward shift — relevant for EM/Argentina-exposure and sovereign-risk positioning.
insights.quote: I have always said that he is going to fix the economy and when it comes to inflation and he did.
